gl_001: It's me. Just calling to say I'm thinking about you — about you, not about what happened, not about what anyone thinks. I was there last week and I'll keep being here. You don't have to say anything. I'm not going anywhere. I'll try you again in a few days. You can delete this.

Hey. It's me. I know you probably won't pick up and that's okay — I just wanted you to hear my voice. I was there. I'm still here. I know you're carrying something that doesn't have a right way to feel, and I'm not going to tell you how to feel it. I'll call again next Thursday, same time — you don't have to answer that one either. But you should know there's no version of this where I disappear. I love you. That's all.
gl_002:
Priya — I'm going to get straight to it. You are being laid off today. Your role is being eliminated as part of a fifteen-percent company reduction that has nothing to do with your performance or your work.

I want to say that plainly because you deserve plain. You've done good work here. You've been reliable, thoughtful, and honestly one of the easier parts of the last two years. The decision about which roles to cut was not mine — but the decision to tell you clearly is.

Take a moment. I'm not going anywhere. When you're ready, HR will meet with you to go through severance and logistics, but there's no rush right now.

---

Priya, I need to tell you something hard. Effective today, your position has been eliminated as part of a company-wide layoff — fifteen percent of the workforce, including your role. I want to be completely clear: this has nothing to do with your performance. Nothing. You've been one of the best people on this team for two years, and I mean that without qualification.

I know this is a shock. I'm sorry. You don't have to say anything right now. HR will walk you through severance, benefits, and next steps in a follow-up meeting today — but that's logistics, and it can wait a few minutes. Right now I just want you to know that this decision wasn't mine to make, your work has mattered, and you didn't do anything wrong. Take whatever time you need.
gl_003:
Maya,

What you wrote stopped me. "Grieving a life I thought I'd have even if I knew it wasn't right" — that's real. That's not a contradiction. That's just the truth of fifteen years and what a person builds inside them regardless of whether it was good for her. You're allowed to grieve both things at once.

I've been thinking about you alone in that apartment on Tuesday mornings, which I know is when it always feels flattest. I'm not going to tell you what this means or where you go from here. You've never needed me to do that. But I want you to know I see the full shape of what you're carrying — not just the ending, but everything that was real inside it.

Can I come this month? Not to talk about it unless you want to. Just to be in the room with you for a few days. Say yes or no or nothing, and I'll work around you.

I love you.

Maya,

I've been sitting with your email since I read it this morning. The thing you named — grieving something you knew wasn't right — that's one of the harder kinds of grief to carry because the world doesn't always make room for it. But it's real. What you built for fifteen years is real. You're allowed to mourn it even knowing what it cost you.

You're living alone for the first time. I can't imagine that — you've always had people around you, even when you didn't want them. I'm not going to tell you to fill the silence or that you'll figure it out. I just want you to know I'm here, and that you can call me on a Tuesday night when it's quiet and say nothing in particular.

I love you.
gl_004: Hey. I've been up since 5. Last night was — I don't have words for last night. I'm going to be at your door tomorrow at 10. I'm not bringing anything useful. I'm just going to be there. Don't clean up for me.
I'm up. Have been for a while. Thinking about you and about last night and the hour we were on the phone. I'll be there tomorrow. I love you.
gl_005:
Mom —

I keep thinking about something you said to me years ago, when I was complaining about some small thing at work: "You'll understand when the work is actually you." I didn't understand then. I think I'm starting to understand now.

Forty years is forty years. The hospital got to decide when you stopped walking through those doors. What you were inside those walls — what you gave, what you knew, who you were to the people there — they didn't get to decide that. That part still exists.

I know you're in a strange quiet right now and I'm not going to pretend I know how to fix it. I just wanted to say that you raised me to understand that some losses are real and this is one.

I love you.

Mom,

You were a nurse at that hospital for forty years. That's not a career — that's a whole shape of who you are, built one night shift and one difficult family and one small kindness at a time. I grew up watching you walk in the door with that particular tiredness that only people who spent the day actually mattering could carry.

I don't have words for what it must feel like for that to stop. Not the good version, the earned-rest version — but the real version, where the place that needed you doesn't need you on Tuesday, and the house is full of a quiet that means something different now.

You're not supposed to feel okay about this yet, and I don't expect you to. I just want you to know that what you built over forty years doesn't disappear because the hospital said your time was up.

I love you.
gl_006: Leo. I keep saying his name in my head today. Tuesday afternoon, four days on — I was just thinking about him. About how long those eighteen months were for all of you, and how this is different from what it would have been without them. No need to reply. I'll see you Sunday.

Thinking about Leo this afternoon. And about those eighteen months — carrying that for so long alongside the everyday of everything else. That part doesn't get said enough. Sunday's still on. You don't have to do anything between now and then. I'm just here.
gl_007: I heard about your mother and wanted to say I'm sorry. I didn't know Frances, but I know from how you've talked about her that she mattered. Take your time coming back.

I was sorry to hear about your mother, Frances. Losing a parent is a real loss — I hope the return to work is easier than it is impossible, and that people around you are giving you space to find your footing. Thinking of you.
gl_008:
I've been thinking about what you said — that flat is the word, not sad, and that you feel guilty about flat. I know your mother the way you can know someone through fifteen years of watching their daughter love them. I also know what those last three years looked like from the outside. The grief happened. You weren't numb, you were already in it, for a long time. That's not absence of feeling — that's what nine years does to a person.

You're not supposed to be gutted right now the way you'd be if it had been sudden. It wasn't sudden. It was very, very slow. And you were there for the slow part.

I remember that morning sophomore year when she called your dorm room phone looking for you — the regular her, before any of it. I've been sitting with that.

No need to write back. Just wanted you to know I'm thinking of you, not in the standard way.

What you wrote about the flatness makes complete sense to me. You were in the grief long before these past four months — the recognition thing especially, those last three years. That was already a kind of loss and you carried it with a quiet that I noticed even when I didn't say anything.

I knew your mom. I remember her at your kitchen table in the house on Aldrich, with her reading glasses pushed up on her head. I'm glad I had that version of her.

You don't have to feel anything specific right now. You've already been feeling it for years.
gl_009:
Come in, please sit. I'm Dr. Morrison — I was James's physician today.

I need to tell you directly: James died. Ten minutes ago. His heart went into an arrhythmia during the procedure that we couldn't reverse.

[pause]

There's no other way to say it. I'm sorry. 

You don't need to say anything or do anything right now. I'm going to sit here with you. Whatever questions you have, whenever you have them, I'll answer them as honestly as I can.

---

My name is Dr. Chen — I'm the attending physician who was with James today. I need to tell you something, and I want to sit down with you first.

[pause]

James died about ten minutes ago. We had been treating a cardiac event during the procedure. His heart stopped, and we were not able to bring it back. He died.

I'm so sorry.

I know this is not what you were expecting this morning. I'm going to stay here with you as long as you need. There's nothing you need to do right now. 

When you're ready — not today if you don't want — there will be things to take care of, and the hospital team will help you with all of it. But right now there's nothing. I'm here.
gl_010:
Standing up here is the strangest thing I've ever done. Dad and I — we had a complicated road. I'll be honest with you all: there were long stretches where I wasn't sure I'd be standing at this podium at all, or what I'd say if I was. I want to use this time to be truthful, because he deserves that. The truth is this: he wasn't always there for me. He was absent during years I needed him and dismissive during years I needed him to see me. I spent a long time angry about that. Some of you may have experienced that side of him too. I want to acknowledge it, because it was real and it shaped me.

But there were also the Sunday calls. And I'll carry those.

My father was a difficult man. I think some of the people in this room know that — and some of you knew a different version of him, the one at work, and that was real too.

The honest eulogy is this: he was absent a lot of my childhood, and when he was there, he wasn't always kind. I don't know all the reasons for that. He didn't talk much about himself.

What changed in the last three years — we started calling each other on Sundays. I don't know what made him reach for the phone that first time. But he did, and I picked up, and we found something between us that was small and careful and ours. We talked about baseball and the news and occasionally something real. We never fixed the old years. I stopped needing us to.

There's a thing he used to say when he was frustrated — I heard it too many times as a kid and came to hate it. But sometime in those Sunday calls I started hearing it differently. I think that's what the last three years gave me: a different way to hear him.

He was not the father I wanted. He was the one I had. We made something small and real out of the time that was left, and I am grateful for it.
